Then, you've got the segments that will run to the two cities, which include several more tunnels, albeit much, much shorter. In total, the cross-border section features 162 km of galleries and tunnels, including shafts and safety bypasses.
Access tunnels have already been built in both of these locations, so it's now a case of joining them together, which, as you might guess, is not as easy as it sounds.
And what's the diameter of the cutting head of this TBM? Uh this TBM is 10.4 m excavated diameter. 2,000 t in nominal mode. Yeah. Half of this weight is at the moment is at the front.
Now, the machine that's going to be setting off here quite soon is pretty different from the one we saw earlier. Basically, it's what we call gripper TBM.

At some points there were 600 m of solid rock above my head, equivalent to the heights of Merdeka 118, the world's second tallest building. Heading into the tunnels you encounter all kinds of signs that this is an extreme environment, including a strict protocol where people are counted in and out of the surface and have to wear location tags in case the worst happens.
It is absolutely remarkable. I'm stood under a mountain. It doesn't really bear thinking about. There's all that stuff above my head.

Four vertical shafts are under construction which will go all the way from the surface, 1,300 m above sea level, to the base tunnel under the mountain. They provide ventilation which is especially important when you're tens of kilometers inside a mountain.

Without it the air wouldn't be breathable, either for the people working on the projects or if passengers need to be evacuated once the tunnels in service. First, a pilot hole roughly 40 cm in diameter is bored into the ground just big enough for a long metal bar to be slotted in all the way down to the tunnel.

Where cavities occur a cylindrical metal formwork is placed inside allowing the gaps to be filled with concrete. Then a cutting head is attached to the bottom which is used to excavate back up to the surface.

While most of the activity on this project has been happening in France, things have started to ramp up on the Italian side as well. Preparations are in place for the facilities that will handle the making of the tunnel segments as well as the processing of the excavated material.

They're unhappy with the environmental damage that they feel the new tunnel will cause, especially as there's already a railway connecting Lyon and Turin. So in other words, they feel it's not really needed. That's despite the new line being a lot faster, promising green benefits, and having less vulnerability to further landslides.

When you visit here today you can clearly see that this is a mega build in full swing. But that hasn't always been the case. There have been delays, opposition from politicians as well as protesters, and financial uncertainty caused by gaps in the budget.
And we are awarding right now the final global contracts for the the equipments. So we are now construction all the civil works.

longSentences.text: France and Italy are two of the largest economies on the planet, and they're situated right next to each other. But despite that, and the fact they're both part of the EU, moving people and goods between them isn't exactly straightforward.

It's a difficult place to build a road, let alone a railway, which is why 92% of all freight movement between Italy and France is done by truck. Now, I probably don't need to tell you that road transportation is both worse for the environment and slower than going by train. So, something is finally being done about it.

But it means going to some pretty extreme lengths. Now, as I said earlier, there already is a way to get from Lyon to Turin by train, as long as you don't mind traveling on a line that was built in the 1800s and is nowhere near high-speed, because it's full of winding curves and steep slopes.

So, this is the start of the massive Mont Cenis Base Tunnel on the French side. Trains are going to enter through a rectangular-shaped cut-and-cover section down there, and then come into this 3-km section, which passes through some particularly tough geology.

The process is quite simple. You basically drill holes in a circle, fill them with dynamite, hit a big red button and blow them apart, clear the rubble out of the way using trucks, then put some steel ribs in to reinforce the rock around you, then spray that with shotcrete, kind of like a mix of sprayed concrete, basically, which you can see around me right now.

is you actually get to see your adversary. You get to see the rock in front of you, the mountain that you're battling. >> >> More holes, more explosions, more rubble cleared away, more shotcrete, and then the same again, step by step, bit by bit.

You can see ahead of me here, they're doing this process, this waterproof membrane going in, this kind of thick sheet on the walls, followed by that kind of brownish-red steel reinforcement. That's going to sit inside the concrete. And then following that, that thick concrete slab.

Another thing that's very visible down here is how wet everything becomes before a section of the tunnel is fully waterproofed. So, it does a lot more than just dig a big hole. It's essentially like a giant mobile factory, which is why it requires an entire team to operate it. There are 15 people all working together to run and supervise this machine's various functions.
